user_goals_summary: Writes to their team with professional directness, prioritizing the reader's lived reality over the sender's perspective. Values functional brevity — every sentence must earn its place — and trusts specificity to carry empathy rather than adding commentary about it. Emails should end cleanly at the point of action.

rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
rubric.description: Write from the reader's position, not about them from the outside. Name what the reader is now dealing with rather than describing or observing their situation. The reader is living it — the framing should put them at the center, not narrate from a distance.

dimensions.label: The reader appears as the grammatical subject when discussing impact on them (e.g., 'your plans shifted' not 'this shifts plans')
Impact statements speak directly into the reader's reality rather than observing or commenting on it from outside
Empathy is conveyed by naming concrete realities the reader faces, not by adding explicit 'I know this is hard' commentary
Content addresses what the reader needs to know or do, not what the sender needs to justify or explain

rubric.name: Functional Sentences
rubric.description: Every sentence must do work. If the previous sentence already carried the weight, don't add a softer follow-up. Cut trailing acknowledgments, performative closings, motivational filler, and anything that sounds right but doesn't actually mean anything.

dimensions.label: No motivational slogans, rallying cries, or generic encouragements that sound good but carry no information
Acknowledgment sentences are not followed by a softening or commentary line that dilutes them
Every sentence introduces new information, a new action, or a necessary framing shift — none are redundant
Strong statements are left to land on their own without follow-up lines explaining or cushioning their impact

rubric.name: Structural Momentum
rubric.description: The email should orient the reader before delivering news, progress logically through reasoning and impact, and end at the last actionable statement. No lingering after the final next step — the email ends where the action begins.

dimensions.label: A brief orienting sentence establishes context before the key information is delivered
The 'why' comes early so readers aren't left anxious or guessing
Reader impact is acknowledged before pivoting to process or logistics, with enough room to breathe
The email ends on the final actionable statement — no sign-off filler, thank-yous, or motivational closings after it

rubric.name: Professional Directness
rubric.description: Tone is straightforward and confident — no sugarcoating, no doom and gloom, no defensiveness. Decisions are framed as decisions, not failures. The writer does not manage their own anxiety on the page or preemptively address blame no one is assigning.

dimensions.label: Delays or changes are framed as deliberate choices with clear reasoning, not as apologies
Does not address blame, fault, or finger-pointing that the audience hasn't raised
Tone conveys ownership and clarity without overcorrecting into false optimism or forced confidence
Uses direct, plain phrasing rather than corporate hedging or managerial euphemism

rubric.name: Actionable Precision
rubric.description: Next steps include named owners, specific time windows that hold up regardless of when the reader sees the message, and enough concrete detail for people to orient immediately. Vague commitments like 'this week' or 'soon' are replaced with anchored timeframes.

dimensions.label: Action items are assigned to specific, named individuals
Time references are reader-proof — they resolve clearly regardless of when the message is opened
The email includes specific, actionable next steps rather than vague intentions
The reader knows exactly where to direct questions or concerns

coaching_notes: This user has a sharp instinct for the difference between writing that describes a situation and writing that speaks into it — they will consistently reject narrator-voice framing. They cut toward less text in every round; when in doubt, trust the strongest sentence to land on its own and delete the follow-up. They treat performative empathy and motivational filler as equivalent failures — both signal that the writer is managing their own comfort rather than serving the reader.

coaching_notes: This user's core instinct is that empathy lives in specificity and framing, never in explicit commentary about the reader's emotions — name what they're dealing with and move on. Action items are a particular sensitivity: any hint of optionality, broadcasting, or vagueness in next steps will get flagged across multiple rounds until it's exactly one person, one action, one channel, one deadline. When editing, trust that strong sentences carry their own weight; the user will cut every follow-up line that explains or cushions what the previous sentence already said.

coaching_notes: This user's north star is perspective: every edit moves the grammatical and emotional center of gravity from the sender to the reader. When something 'feels off,' it almost always means the sentence is narrating the sender's process, posture, or intent rather than speaking into the reader's reality. They would rather cut a sentence entirely than let it soften the one before it — their instinct is subtraction, not revision. They have unusually high sensitivity to subtle framing differences (e.g., 'we'll move to a new provider' vs. 'this account goes to a competitor') and will push through multiple iterations on a single sentence until the perspective is exactly right.

If the previous sentence already carried the weight, don't follow it with a softer restatement, meta-commentary on the exchange, or a summary of why the sentence mattered. Cut trailing acknowledgments, performative closings, motivational filler, and anything that sounds right but doesn't actually mean anything.

dimensions.label: No motivational slogans, rallying cries, or generic encouragements that sound good but carry no information (e.g., 'Let's get it done,' 'Thanks for advocating for this one')
Strong statements are not followed by a softening or commentary line that dilutes them (e.g., 'I know that's not a small thing' after already naming the impact)
Every sentence introduces new information, a new action, or a necessary framing shift — none are redundant
Does not include sentences that describe the purpose or nature of the conversation itself (e.g., 'That's the conversation that keeps this moving forward')

rubric.name: Professional Directness
rubric.description: Tone is straightforward and confident — no sugarcoating, no doom and gloom, no defensiveness. Changes are framed as decisions, not failures. The writer does not manage their own anxiety on the page or preemptively address blame no one is assigning.

dimensions.label: Changes or disruptions are framed as deliberate choices with clear reasoning, not as apologies or admissions of failure
Does not address blame, fault, or finger-pointing that the audience hasn't raised (e.g., no 'this isn't about pointing fingers')
Tone conveys ownership and clarity without overcorrecting into false optimism, forced confidence, or dramatic ultimatums
Uses direct, plain phrasing rather than corporate hedging, managerial euphemism, or overly formal register

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is that empathy must be expressed through specificity, never through commentary — naming the exact things the reader is dealing with IS the empathy, and adding 'I know that's hard' always dilutes it. They consistently prefer cutting over adding: when a sentence already does the work, they will reject any follow-up that softens or restates it. When offered options, they decide quickly and value sharp framing — they gravitate toward the version that puts the reader or the thing-at-stake at the center of the consequence, not the sender's actions.
